subject: ap-government
title: Quantitative Analysis — Partisan Attitudes on Compromise
stimulus: Source: American National Election Studies 2016 Pilot, January 2016. Bar graph showing the percentage of Democrats, Republicans, and Independents who believe elected officials should compromise versus stick to their principles. Key data: approximately 55% of Republicans believe officials should stick to principles compared to approximately 34% of Democrats. Democrats show higher support for compromise than Republicans.

parts.prompt: Identify the political affiliation of people who are most likely to believe elected officials should compromise.
parts.scoring_notes: Only Democrats earns the point. Republicans or Independents do not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Identify the political affiliation of people who are most likely to believe elected officials should compromise.

required_elements: Identifies Democrats as the political affiliation most likely to believe in compromise, as shown by the data
alternatives.correct_example: According to the bar graph, Democrats are most likely to believe that elected officials should compromise.

wrong_examples: Republicans — the data shows Republicans are more likely to believe officials should stick to their principles, not compromise.
Independents — the data shows Independents fall between Democrats and Republicans, but Democrats show the highest support for compromise.
common_traps: The answer must be supported by the data in the graph — only Democrats earns the point
Do not accept 'everyone' or 'most Americans' — the question asks for a specific political affiliation

parts.prompt: Describe the difference between Democrats and Republicans on their attitudes of whether government officials should stick to their principles, based on the data in the bar graph.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any accurate description of the partisan difference on this attitude. The response must use the data, not just describe general partisan differences.

scoring_points.description: Describe the difference between Democrats and Republicans on their attitudes of whether government officials should stick to their principles, based on the data in the bar graph.

required_elements: Accurately describes the partisan difference using the data — more Republicans than Democrats believe officials should stick to principles
alternatives.correct_example: A higher percentage of Republicans (approximately 55%) compared to Democrats (approximately 34%) believe that elected officials should stick to their principles rather than compromise.

required_elements: Accurately describes the partisan difference using the data
alternatives.correct_example: Republicans are more likely than Democrats to want elected officials to stick to their principles — about 55% of Republicans hold this view compared to about 34% of Democrats, a roughly 21-percentage-point difference.

wrong_examples: Democrats are more principled than Republicans — this misinterprets the data; the graph shows Republicans prefer sticking to principles.
Describing only one party's attitude without comparing to the other.
common_traps: The response must compare both parties — not just describe one
Approximate percentages from the graph earn the point; students are not expected to read exact values

parts.prompt: Explain how the data in the bar graph could influence how a Republican candidate would shift his or her campaign positions after securing the Republican nomination for president.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain the specific shift from primary to general election strategy based on the data. Generic statements about candidates changing positions do not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the data in the bar graph could influence how a Republican candidate would shift his or her campaign positions after securing the Republican nomination for president.

required_elements: Explains the primary-to-general election shift strategy based on the partisan attitude data
Must reference how the candidate would adjust positions to appeal to a broader electorate
alternatives.correct_example: During the primary, a Republican candidate would emphasize sticking to conservative principles to appeal to Republican primary voters, who the data shows prefer officials who do not compromise. After securing the nomination, the candidate would shift toward a more moderate, compromise-friendly message to appeal to Independent and Democratic voters who are more supportive of compromise.

wrong_examples: The candidate would keep the same positions throughout — does not reflect how candidates adjust strategy after the primary.
Describing how the Democrat candidate would change positions — the question specifically asks about the Republican candidate.
common_traps: The response must connect the specific data (Republican preference for principle-keeping vs. Democratic/Independent preference for compromise) to the strategic shift
The key insight is the primary-general election dynamic: appeal to base in primary, then moderate for the general election

parts.prompt: Explain how the data in the bar graph could affect policy making interactions between the president and Congress.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must connect the data about partisan attitudes to a specific effect on president-Congress policymaking interactions. Accept any plausible and accurate explanation grounded in the data.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the data in the bar graph could affect policy making interactions between the president and Congress.

required_elements: Connects the partisan attitude data to a specific effect on how the president and Congress interact in policymaking
alternatives.correct_example: If a Republican president and Republican Congress both prioritize sticking to principles over compromise, policymaking would be easier when government is unified — but in times of divided government, the large partisan gap in attitudes toward compromise could cause significant gridlock since Republicans and Democrats both represent their base preferences.

required_elements: Connects the partisan attitude data to a specific effect on president-Congress interactions
alternatives.correct_example: The data suggests that unified government (same party controlling the presidency and Congress) would make policymaking easier, while divided government would be especially difficult because Republicans who prefer sticking to principles and Democrats who prefer compromise would be unable to find middle ground.

wrong_examples: Simply stating that partisanship affects policymaking — must connect the specific data about attitudes to a concrete effect on presidential-congressional interactions.
Describing how elections work without connecting to policymaking.
common_traps: The response must connect the data to policymaking specifically between the president and Congress
Accept explanations about unified government, divided government, gridlock, or compromise capacity as long as they are grounded in the partisan attitude data
